message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    An epidemic model based only on respiratory droplets and close contact could not fully explain the regional differences in the spread of the recent severe acute respiratory syndrome COVID-19 in Italy, which was fast and dramatic only in Lombardy and Po Valley. On March 16
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    2020, we presented a Position Paper proposing a research hypothesis concerning the association between higher mortality rates due to COVID-19 observed in Northern Italy and the peaks of particulate matter concentrations, frequently exceeding the legal limit of 50 µg/m
                    <jats:sup>3</jats:sup>
                    as PM
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    daily average
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    To assess environmental factors related to the spread of the COVID-19 in Italy from February 24
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    to March 13
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    (the date when the lockdown has been imposed over Italy), official daily data relevant to ambient PM
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    levels were collected from all Italian Provinces between February 9
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    and February 29
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    , taking into account the average time (estimated in 17 days) elapsed between the initial infection and the recorded COVID positivity. In addition to the number of exceedances of PM
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    daily limit value, we considered also population data and daily travelling information per each Province.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    PM
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    daily limit value exceedances appear to be a significant predictor (p < .001) of infection in univariate analyses. Less polluted Provinces had a median of 0.03 infection cases over 1000 residents, while most polluted Provinces had a median of 0.26 cases over 1000 residents. Thirty-nine out of 41 Northern Italian Provinces resulted in the category with highest PM
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    levels, while 62 out of 66 Southern Provinces presented low PM
                    <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                    concentrations (p< 0.001). In Milan, the average growth rate before the lockdown was significantly higher than Rome (0.34 vs. 0.27 per day, with a doubling time of 2.0 days vs. 2.6), suggesting a basic reproductive number R
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    >6.0, comparable with the highest values estimated for China.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: The Potential role of Particulate Matter in the Spreading of COVID-19 in Northern Italy: First Evidence-based Research Hypotheses
